The work seeks to explain how a maximal signal speed (relational analogue to light cone) might arise dynamically from N-body system geometry.
AI.method: Analyzed scale-invariant N-body dynamics on shape space (configuration space modulo translations/rotations/dilations). Used affine parametrization as a gauge choice for perturbative analysis. Derived second-order equations for linear perturbations around central configurations, examined their high-frequency spectral properties to identify c_rel as an emergent light-cone velocity.
AI.result: Discovered that under conditions of reparametrization invariance, spectral universality, and hyperbolicity, the spectral constant c_rel provides Lorentzian structure to the product space ℝ×S. This suggests causal structure and maximal signal speed emerge from relational configuration space geometry and spectra.
AI.conclusion: Relativistic kinematics' core features (light cone, causal structure) may not require a priori spacetime but can instead dynamically emerge from N-body relational dynamics on shape space. This points to a new foundation for spacetime structure rooted in configuration geometry rather than being fundamental.

title: Transient X-ray Sources as Extremely Eccentric Mass-Transfer Binaries with Compact Companions
summary: It has long been suggested that X-ray transients are produced at periastron of stellar-compact object binaries with eccentric orbits. Recoil of matter evaporated from the star by X-rays from matter transferred at periastron increases the orbital semi-major axis and eccentricity. After periastrons the object would be a transient X-ray source, a Galactic analogue of a tidal disruption event (TDE), but recurrent with a gradually increasing period rather than catastrophic.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes that X-ray transients in eccentric stellar-compact object binaries result from periastron passage, where X-ray-driven evaporation of stellar matter causes gradual orbital expansion and increased eccentricity, creating recurrent transient events analogous to TDEs but non-catastrophic.
AI.motivation: To explain the origin of X-ray transients with increasing orbital periods, offering an alternative to the traditional tidal disruption event (TDE) model by proposing a recurrent, non-catastrophic mechanism.
AI.method: Analyzes the orbital dynamics of eccentric binaries involving a compact object, focusing on periastron interactions where X-ray emission evaporates stellar matter. Computes the recoil effect from evaporated material to model semi-major axis and eccentricity evolution over successive orbits.
AI.result: Shows that X-ray-driven ablation introduces a feedback mechanism where each periastron passage slightly expands the orbit and increases eccentricity, leading to spaced-out transient events with gradually longer intervals between episodes.
AI.conclusion: Such systems could naturally produce recurring X-ray transients with evolving orbital periods, providing a Galactic-scale precedent to TDEs without requiring complete stellar disruption.

title: Very Heavy and Composite Dark Matter: Theory and Experimental Searches
summary: Dark matter much heavier than the weak scale remains a comparatively unexplored frontier. This review surveys theoretical and experimental developments on very heavy dark matter, including composite and dissipative formation mechanisms, multiscatter detection, and astrophysical searches.

AI.tldr: This review examines theoretical and experimental aspects of very heavy dark matter, covering formation mechanisms like composite and dissipative, detection via multiscatter methods, and astrophysical search strategies.
AI.motivation: Dark matter particles heavier than the weak scale are underexplored, necessitating a comprehensive review of existing theories and detection methods to guide future research and experiments.
AI.method: The paper surveys existing literature on very heavy dark matter, analyzing formation mechanisms (composite/dissipative), detection techniques (multiscatter), and astrophysical search methods.
AI.result: Highlights key developments in understanding heavy dark matter properties and detection, emphasizing areas needing further exploration and interdisciplinary collaboration between particle physics and astrophysics.
AI.conclusion: Underscores the importance of expanding research into very heavy dark matter to address cosmological puzzles and calls for advanced experimental setups to probe this mass regime.

title: Conservative cosmology in scalar-tensor Herglotz $f(R,T)$ gravity
comment: 17 pages, 3 figures, to appear in Classical and Quantum Gravity (CQG)
summary: The scalar-tensor representation of $f(R,T)$ gravity is extended to incorporate the Herglotz variational principle. The field equations are derived in both the geometric and scalar-tensor frameworks. Although the divergence of the energy-momentum tensor in matter-geometry coupling theories is generally nonvanishing, conservation can be achieved through the introduction of the Herglotz vector. The generalized Friedmann equations in scalar-tensor Herglotz $f(R,T)$ theory are obtained, and a conservative cosmological model is shown to be consistent with late-time observational data. Comparisons with analogous nonconservative models and with the standard $Λ$CDM model are also provided.

AI.tldr: The paper extends $f(R,T)$ gravity using the Herglotz variational principle, leading to a conservative cosmological model consistent with observations.
AI.motivation: To address the issue of nonconservation in matter-geometry coupling theories and derive a consistent cosmological model that matches late-time observations.
AI.method: Extended the scalar-tensor framework of $f(R,T)$ gravity with the Herglotz variational principle, derived field equations in both geometric and scalar-tensor forms, and applied the Herglotz vector to ensure energy-momentum conservation.
AI.result: Obtained generalized Friedmann equations, demonstrated a conservative cosmological model compatible with observational data, and compared it with nonconservative models and ΛCDM.
AI.conclusion: The incorporation of Herglotz principle effectively maintains conservation laws, providing a viable alternative cosmological model to ΛCDM and nonconservative theories.

title: Annihilation of Secluded Dark Matter into W+W- Enhanced by P-wave Sommerfeld Effect
summary: We propose that a pair of annihilation of the secluded dark matter may accommodate a source of the halo gamma ray signal reported recently, through the p-wave Sommerfeld enhancement. We show that given a weakly coupling of the dark sector to the Higgs bosons, the dark matter annihilation into W+W-, even though induced at 1-loop level, is desirably amplified. We also argue that the supersymmetric framework may readily embody such a model within rather minimal contents.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes that the annihilation of secluded dark matter pairs, enhanced via p-wave Sommerfeld effect, can explain recent halo gamma-ray signals. A weak Higgs coupling in the dark sector amplifies loop-induced W+W- annihilation, and a minimal supersymmetric model accommodates this mechanism.
AI.motivation: To explain the observed halo gamma-ray signals through dark matter annihilation mechanisms involving Sommerfeld enhancement and loop-induced processes.
AI.method: The authors analyze the conditions under which secluded dark matter annihilation, mediated by p-wave Sommerfeld enhancement, produces detectable gamma rays. They consider a weakly coupled dark sector interacting via Higgs bosons and study 1-loop level annihilation into W+/- pairs within a supersymmetric framework.
AI.result: The model successfully explains the gamma-ray signals with a suppressed thermal relic density, achievable through the interplay of Sommerfeld enhancement and loop suppression. A minimal supersymmetric setup validates this scenario.
AI.conclusion: Secluded dark matter with Higgs-mediated interactions and p-wave Sommerfeld enhancement provides a viable explanation for observed halo gamma rays, realizable in a simple supersymmetric model.

title: Freezing lakes as analogue models of $Λ$CDM cosmology and beyond
summary: We extend previous conduction-based analogies between ice growth in a lake and cosmological expansion by incorporating buoyancy-driven heat transport. Reformulating the Stefan problem with both conductive and convective fluxes yields an evolution equation for the ice thickness $s(t)$ that is structurally analogous to the Friedmann equations for the cosmological scale factor $a(t)$. Beyond reproducing radiation-, matter-, and curvature-like behaviors, we introduce a reduced description of convection in which the vertically integrated heat flux reaching the moving ice-water interface is modeled as a power-law function of the instantaneous liquid-layer thickness, generating two additional effective contributions. The first is a constant term, directly analogous to a cosmological constant, arising from the persistence of buoyancy-driven transport under geometric confinement. The second is a $s^{-1}$ contribution originating from the coupling between the moving ice boundary and the convective boundary layer. This term reflects the specific reduced flux-height Ansatz adopted, rather than a universal physical prediction. When expressed in Friedmann-like cosmological form, this term entails a fluid with negative energy density and equation-of-state parameter $w=-2/3$. In cosmology this term may be an effective one associated to a network of domain walls made of exotic energy/matter, but it might also arise from an energy exchange between cosmological components. Overall, the results should be interpreted as a structural analogy between evolution equations, showing how nonlinear transport mechanisms in a classical moving-boundary problem can reproduce the hierarchy of scaling terms familiar from cosmology within a reduced and analytically tractable framework.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an analogy between ice growth in a lake and cosmological expansion by incorporating buoyancy-driven convection. The ice thickness evolution equation mirrors the Friedmann equations, including terms analogous to a cosmological constant and a negative energy density fluid with w = -2/3, akin to domain walls or energy exchange mechanisms. This establishes a structural analogy between nonlinear heat transport and cosmic expansion dynamics.
AI.motivation: To extend previous conduction-based models of lake ice growth to better mimic cosmological expansion dynamics by including convective heat transport, allowing the derivation of evolution equations structurally similar to cosmological models.
AI.method: Reformulating the Stefan problem to include both conductive and convective heat fluxes. A reduced model of convection treats heat flux as a power-law function of the liquid layer thickness, introducing two effective terms analogous to cosmological parameters. These terms are analytically derived and interpreted within a Friedmann-like framework.
AI.result: The model reproduces radiation-, matter-, and curvature-like behaviors. Two novel contributions emerge: a constant term (cosmological constant analogue) and a s^{-1} term (negative energy density fluid with w=-2/3). The latter arises specifically from the convective boundary layer coupling, suggesting mechanisms like domain walls or energy exchange in cosmology.
AI.conclusion: The analogy demonstrates how classical heat transport mechanisms in a moving boundary problem can mirror cosmological scaling terms. This provides an analytically tractable system for understanding nonlinear transport phenomena and their structural parallels to cosmic expansion, though the specific terms depend on the chosen model assumptions.

title: Realistic Equations of State Informing Neutron Star Post-Merger Gravitational-Wave Frequencies
summary: Binary neutron star mergers are thought to produce hot, rapidly rotating neutron stars with masses that can far exceed their Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff mass. The gravitational-wave emission from such remnants provides a unique opportunity to measure the nuclear equation of state at densities and temperatures not available to terrestrial experiments. Current detector design is informed by gravitational-wave signals from general relativistic hydrodynamics simulations of neutron star mergers, typically with hybrid thermal treatments for the equation of state, where a cold equation of state is modified by adding a thermal component. We use realistic equations of state based on the relativistic mean field model with consistent treatment of thermal effects to compute the distribution of expected peak gravitational-wave frequencies. Marginalising over equation of state and progenitor neutron star masses, we show the peak frequency of emission ranges from $\sim2.5$ to 4 kHz. The width of this distribution suggests the need for broadband observatories with kHz sensitivity, and calls into question some of the so-called post-merger optimised configurations. We show the proposed KAGRA high-frequency design is well-suited to measuring post-merger remnants when compared to the KAGRA broadband design.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the gravitational-wave signals from binary neutron star mergers using realistic equations of state with thermal treatments, finding peak frequencies between 2.5-4 kHz, which underscores the need for broadband detectors like the KAGRA high-frequency design.
AI.motivation: To understand the nuclear equation of state under extreme conditions and improve detector designs for capturing gravitational waves from post-merger remnants.
AI.method: Using relativistic mean field models with consistent thermal treatments in hydrodynamics simulations to compute gravitational-wave peak frequencies.
AI.result: Peak frequencies range from ~2.5 to 4 kHz, indicating the necessity of broadband detectors and questioning some optimized configurations.
AI.conclusion: The KAGRA high-frequency design is better suited than broadband configurations for measuring post-merger remnants due to the identified frequency range.

title: Spin effects in the tau-lepton pair induced by anomalous magnetic and electric dipole moments
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures; contribution to Proceedings of the XLVI International Conference of Theoretical Physics "Matter to the Deepest", Katowice, Poland, 15-19 September, 2025
summary: The possible anomalous New Physics contributions to magnetic and electric dipole moments of the $τ$ lepton have brought renewed interest in studying $τ$-pair production at energies of the LHC and future colliders. We discuss effects of electromagnetic and weak dipole moment contributions to the $τ$-lepton polarization and $ττ$ spin correlations in the $γγ\to τ^-τ^+$ and $q \bar{q} \to τ^- τ^+$ processes. Such processes have been observed in $pp$ and PbPb collisions in the LHC experiments. Extensions of the Standard Model amplitudes for $γγ\to τ^-τ^+$ and $q \bar{q} \to τ^- τ^+$ processes, which include dipole moments of the $τ$ lepton, are implemented in the TauSpinner Monte Carlo program. A few examples of signatures of $ττ$ spin correlations and $τ$-lepton dipole moments in observables are presented.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how new physics effects in the τ lepton's dipole moments influence τ-pair production processes at high-energy colliders, using Monte Carlo simulations and presenting signatures for detecting these effects in observables.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to investigate anomalous contributions from New Physics to the τ lepton's magnetic and electric dipole moments, leveraging τ-pair production at LHC and future colliders to detect deviations from Standard Model predictions.
AI.method: The authors extend Standard Model amplitudes for γγ→τ⁻τ⁺ and q antique→τ⁻τ⁺ processes with τ lepton dipole moments. These are implemented in the TauSpinner Monte Carlo program to analyze polarization effects and spin correlations in ττ pairs.
AI.result: Examples of signatures demonstrating ττ spin correlations and the impacts of τ dipole moments on observables are provided, highlighting potential deviations from Standard Model expectations that could indicate New Physics.
AI.conclusion: The study underscores the importance of analyzing τ-pair production observables to probe τ lepton dipole moments as a viable avenue for uncovering evidence of physics beyond the Standard Model at current and future collider experiments.

title: The Shape of Eccentricity: Rapid Classification of Eccentric Binaries with the Wavelet Scattering Transform
summary: The gravitational-wave (GW) detections reported by the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA (LVK) collaboration have so far been consistent with quasi-circular compact binary coalescences (CBCs). Nevertheless, a small fraction of binaries driven to merge through dynamical interactions in dense stellar environments or in field triples may retain measurable orbital eccentricity when entering the sensitive frequency band of LVK detectors. Confident measurement of eccentricity in the LVK band would provide strong evidence for such dynamically driven mergers; however, eccentric gravitational-waveform models are computationally expensive, and performing production-level inference on all detected signals is not an efficient use of resources when eccentric signals are expected to be rare. An intermediate step between detection and analysis, in which the signal is assessed for the potential presence of eccentricity, could provide quick recommendations for which signals should undergo full eccentric inference. We apply the wavelet scattering transform (WST) to a large set of synthetic waveforms in realistic noise and assess its discriminatory power using simple linear and shallow neural-network classifiers. We find that the WST representation enables effective discrimination between eccentric and quasi-circular binaries and provides a compact multi-scale representation of GW signals. Our approach achieves ~64% percent detection accuracy at a false alarm rate of 10%, with an AUC of 0.844 and an average precision of 0.876. We also examine the ability of our classifiers to distinguish eccentricity from spin-induced precession and find robust performance across a range of spin-precession magnitudes.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using wavelet scattering transforms (WST) with classifiers to efficiently identify eccentric gravitational-wave signals, which could indicate dynamically driven binary mergers. It achieves ~64% detection accuracy with an AUC of 0.844, showing potential for pre-screening signals before full eccentric inference.
AI.motivation: Eccentric gravitational-wave signals are rare but important for understanding merger origins. Direct analysis with eccentric models is computationally intensive, so the authors aim to develop a fast, efficient pre-screening method to identify candidates needing full eccentric inference.
AI.method: The method applies wavelet scattering transforms to synthetic waveforms in noisy data, then uses linear/shallow neural network classifiers to distinguish eccentric from quasi-circular signals. They also test discrimination against spin-precession effects.
AI.result: Achieved 64% detection accuracy at 10% false alarm rate, AUC 0.844, average precision 0.876. Classifiers effectively differentiated eccentricity from spin precession across varying spin conditions.
AI.conclusion: WST-based classification provides a computationally efficient way to pre-select eccentric candidates. This approach could optimize resource use for LVK's data analysis by focusing full eccentric inference only on likely candidates.

title: The environmental dependence of mid-IR luminous dusty Supernovae
comment: 21 pages, 13 figures, 3 Tables; accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: Using the Spitzer and WISE images, we discovered 42 mid-IR luminous dusty supernovae with local integral-field spectroscopy data. The observed mid-IR emission indicates the presence of newly formed dust, or pre-existing dust heated by the radiation from the supernovae or circumstellar medium interactions. We carried out a systematic analysis of the supernova host environments and their dust properties, for understanding the dust-veiled exploding stars, and whether such an intense dust production process is associated with their local environments. We find that dusty supernovae prefer the locations with higher EW(Hα), lower metallicity, and heavier host extinctions compared to typical SN types, and they show the same increasing sequence in the values of EW(Hα) and oxygen abundance from hydrogen-rich, type IIn and hydrogen-poor dusty supernovae. These differences in environmental properties of different dusty SN types indicate the diversity of their progenitors. We also found that one marginal correlation is a negative correlation between the SN dust mass and star formation rate. This means that SNe would be more mid-IR luminous and more dust-rich at the region with lower star formation rate. However, the SN dust mass show no correlation with the metallicity and the host extinction, which were thought to be key factors affecting the mass-loss history of progenitors and the CSM environment of SNe. Therefore, the dust formation process in SNe might be insensitive to metallicity and the dust condition of their host environments.

AI.tldr: The study uses Spitzer and WISE data to analyze 42 mid-IR luminous dusty supernovae, finding environmental preferences for higher Hα EW, lower metallicity, and higher host extinction. Dust mass shows no correlation with metallicity or host extinction but a negative trend with star formation rate, suggesting SN dust formation is less influenced by local environment factors.
AI.motivation: To understand the dust-veiled supernovae and whether their intense dust production links to local environments, investigating progenitor diversity and environmental influences on dust formation.
AI.method: Analyzing mid-IR emissions from 42 supernovae using Spitzer/WISE imagery, combined with integral-field spectroscopy to assess host galaxy environments (EW(Hα), metallicity, extinction) and dust properties.
AI.result: Dusty SNe prefer higher Hα EW, lower metallicity, and higher host extinction areas. Dust mass negatively correlates with star formation rate but shows no link to metallicity/extinction, indicating metallicity-insensitive dust formation in SNe.
AI.conclusion: SN dust production is influenced by star formation rate but not metallicity or host extinction, suggesting progenitor properties rather than local environment primarily drive dust formation diversity.

title: Impact of non-standard neutrino-electron interactions on Big Bang Nucleosynthesis
summary: Neutrino non-standard interactions (NSI) with electrons, predicted in many extended theoretical models of particle physics, are known to alter the picture of neutrino decoupling from the cosmic plasma. We update previous analyses of neutrino decoupling in presence of NSI with electrons, extending the parameter space in order to provide, for the first time, a full study of their effect on the production of light elements during Big Bang Nucleosynthesis (BBN). We compare the BBN bounds on non-universal and flavour-changing NSI parameters with the constraints from terrestrial experiments. Our results show that the limits from BBN are significantly less stringent than the experimental bounds, but they are complementary and can provide a test of neutrino physics at different temperature scales and epochs.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how neutrino non-standard interactions (NSI) with electrons impact Big Bang Nucleosynthesis (BBN), examining BBN-derived constraints on NSI parameters and comparing them with terrestrial experiment limits.
AI.motivation: To investigate the effects of NSI on neutrino decoupling and light element production during BBN, and to compare cosmological constraints with lab-based experimental bounds.
AI.method: Extended parameter space analysis of NSI with electrons during neutrino decoupling, modeling BBN light element production, and comparing cosmological constraints with terrestrial experiment data.
AI.result: BBN-derived limits on NSI parameters are less stringent but complementary to experimental constraints, offering insights across different temperature scales and epochs.
AI.conclusion: BBN provides a unique cosmological probe for testing neutrino interactions at high temperatures, complementary to Earth-based experiments.

title: An Upper Bound for the Mass of Microscopic Clocks
summary: According to general relativity, clocks are the basic measuring devices needed to probe spacetime geometry. However, it is generally accepted that the mass of clocks capable of measuring small time intervals must be bounded from below. In this article, we consider two gravitationally induced phenomena: first, the extent to which such a mass disturbs the geometry that the clocks intended to probe; second, the magnitude of the gravitational self-interaction. We adopt the semiclassical coupling between gravity and quantum matter in the non-relativistic regime to obtain upper bounds on the mass of the clocks for a given time resolution and running time.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the limitations on the mass of clocks used to measure spacetime geometry due to gravitational effects, deriving upper mass bounds based on semiclassical gravity and quantum matter interactions.
AI.motivation: To address the problem of how the mass of clocks affects their ability to probe spacetime without self-interfering, and to establish upper limits on clock mass for specific time resolution and operation duration.
AI.method: Uses semiclassical coupling between gravity and non-relativistic quantum matter to analyze gravitational disturbances caused by clock mass and its self-interaction, calculating mass bounds.
AI.result: Derives upper bounds on clock mass depending on desired time resolution and running time, showing that heavier clocks perturb spacetime more, limiting their effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: There are fundamental gravitational constraints on clock mass for precise spacetime measurements, requiring careful balance between mass, resolution, and operational time to avoid self-induced geometric disturbances.

title: Search for Gamma-ray emission from Abell 119 galaxy cluster using INTEGRAL/ISGRI, COMPTEL, and DAMPE data
summary: We present a comprehensive search for non-thermal high-energy $γ$-ray emission from the nearby merging galaxy cluster Abell~119 ($z=0.044$) using archival observations spanning over seven decades in photon energy. Our analysis combines hard X-ray data from INTEGRAL/ISGRI (30--100~keV), MeV $γ$-ray observations from COMPTEL (0.75--30~MeV), and GeV--TeV data from the DArk Matter Particle Explorer (DAMPE; 3~GeV--1~TeV). No statistically significant emission is detected at the cluster position in any energy band. In the hard X-ray regime, we derive a $3σ$ upper limit of $F_{30-100\,\mathrm{keV}} \lesssim 8.5 \times 10^{-11}$~erg~cm$^{-2}$~s$^{-1}$ from ISGRI mosaic imaging. Reanalysis of archival COMPTEL data yields 95\% confidence-level upper limits ranging from $\sim 9 \times 10^{-11}$ to $\sim 8 \times 10^{-10}$~erg~cm$^{-2}$~s$^{-1}$ across 0.75--30~MeV. In the GeV--TeV range, DAMPE constrains the differential energy flux to $\sim 10^{-12}$--$10^{-10}$~erg~cm$^{-2}$~s$^{-1}$ (95\% confidence level). These results provide independent multi-band constraints on the reported GeV excess from recent Fermi-LAT studies. While our DAMPE limits do not exclude the flux levels claimed in those analyses, the absence of confirmation across keV--MeV--GeV bands indicate that any non-thermal emission from Abell~119 remains tentative.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a multi-wavelength search for non-thermal γ-ray emission from the galaxy cluster Abell 119 using data from INTEGRAL/ISGRI, COMPTEL, and DAMPE. No significant emission was detected, and upper limits across 30 keV to 1 TeV were established, casting doubt on previously reported GeV excesses from this object.
AI.motivation: To independently validate or refute the reported GeV excess signals in Abell 119 from earlier Fermi-LAT studies through multi-band observations spanning a broader energy range.
AI.method: Analyzed archival data from three instruments: INTEGRAL/ISGRI (30-100 keV), COMPTEL (0.75-30 MeV), and DAMPE (3 GeV-1 TeV). Applied mosaic imaging and reanalysis techniques to derive upper limits in each energy band.
AI.result: No significant non-thermal emission detected in any band. Derived upper limits: 8.5×10⁻¹¹ erg/cm²/s (ISGRI), 9×10⁻¹¹–8×10⁻¹⁰ erg/cm²/s (COMPTEL), and 10⁻¹²–10⁻¹⁰ erg/cm²/s (DAMPE).
AI.conclusion: The absence of multi-band confirmation suggests any non-thermal emission in Abell 119 remains unconfirmed. DAMPE constraints are consistent but do not support earlier GeV claims, indicating possible instrumental effects or astrophysical uncertainties in previous results.

title: Probing the Dark Matter EFT with QUEST-DMC: Projected Sensitivities and Attenuation Ceilings
comment: Proceedings contributions to Corfu 2025, 12 pages, 3 figs, 1 table
summary: This proceedings contribution summarises projected constraints from the QUEST-DMC concept, a surface-based direct-detection experiment using superfluid $^3$He operated below the millikelvin regime and instrumented with nanomechanical resonators read out by SQUIDs. The low recoil-energy threshold (down to sub-eV for the SQUID configuration) enables sensitivity to sub-GeV dark matter across a wide set of interaction structures beyond the canonical spin-independent and spin-dependent limits. We present projections in the non-relativistic Effective Field Theory (EFT) framework, scanning the standard set of fourteen Galilean-invariant operators and expressing reach in terms of effective dark matter-nucleon (or dark matter-neutron) cross sections. Because QUEST-DMC operates at the surface, we also account for suppression of the incident flux due to scattering in the atmosphere and Earth, which produces an interaction-dependent sensitivity ceiling at large couplings. Finally, we outline how the non-relativistic results map onto representative relativistic EFT dark matter-nucleon bilinears, enabling a compact interpretation of the projected reach in terms of UV-motivated coupling structures.

AI.tldr: The QUEST-DMC experiment aims to detect sub-GeV dark matter using superfluid helium and SQUIDs, offering sensitivity across various interaction types and accounting for atmospheric/Earth flux suppression.
AI.motivation: To explore dark matter interactions beyond standard spin-dependent/independent models and access sub-GeV mass ranges with low recoil energy thresholds.
AI.method: Uses superfluid $^3$He at millikelvin temperatures with nanomechanical resonators/SQUIDs for detection. Applies non-relativistic EFT with 14 Galilean-invariant operators and accounts for atmospheric/Earth flux suppression in projections.
AI.result: Projected sensitivity extends to sub-eV recoil energies, showing interaction-dependent constraints across 14 EFT operators. Results mapped to relativistic EFT bilinears for UV model interpretation.
AI.conclusion: QUEST-DMC provides unique probes of dark matter couplings at low energies, filling gaps in parameter space not covered by existing experiments through its surface-based design and comprehensive EFT analysis.

title: Dynamics of spinning particles in pp-wave spacetimes
summary: In this work, we study the dynamics of a spinning particle in pp-waves spacetimes; in particular, plane gravitational waves and impulsive shockwaves. W pay special attention to analytical considerations; this is possible due to an appropriate choice of the spin supplementary condition, various Hamiltonian formalisms (including a non-minimal one) and constants of motion associated with conformal fields. Based on these results, we establish a relation between the motions of a spinning particle in pp-waves and electromagnetic fields suggested by a gauge-gravity duality.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the motion of spinning particles in pp-wave spacetimes, including plane gravitational waves and impulsive shockwaves, using analytical methods with specific spin conditions and Hamiltonian frameworks, and links their dynamics to electromagnetic fields through gauge-gravity duality.
AI.motivation: To understand the behavior of spinning particles in dynamic spacetimes like pp-waves, which are important in gravitational physics and gauge-gravity duality, by exploiting analytical tools and supplementary conditions that allow exact solutions.
AI.method: Analytical approaches using selected spin supplementary conditions (e.g., Pirani or Tulczyjew), Hamiltonian formalisms (including non-minimal couplings), and constants of motion derived from conformal field symmetries. The study connects these particle motions to electromagnetic phenomena via gauge-gravity duality principles.
AI.result: Establishes a relationship between spinning particle trajectories in pp-waves and analogous motions in electromagnetic fields, suggesting deeper connections between gravitational and gauge theories through analytical solutions.
AI.conclusion: The analysis demonstrates that pp-wave spacetimes provide a valuable testbed for exploring spinning particle dynamics and gauge-gravity duality, offering insights into both gravitational physics and high-energy electromagnetic interactions.

title: X-ray Quasi-Periodic Oscillations in Active Galactic Nuclei and Their Implications for the Changing Look Phenomenon
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures, 1 table, accepted to A&A
summary: X-ray timing of active galactic nuclei (AGN) provides a unique probe of gas accretion onto supermassive black holes (SMBHs). Quasi-periodic oscillations (QPOs), which trace gas dynamics in the strongly curved spacetime around SMBHs, are rare in AGN. These signals often are analogs of high-frequency QPOs occasionally seen in some black-hole X-ray binaries, and their scarcity in AGN can partly be attributed to the low frequencies expected for typical SMBH masses. Intriguingly, robust X-ray QPO detections in SMBH systems have so far been reported only in narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxies (NLS1s) and tidal disruption events (TDEs). Here we report the discovery of a QPO candidate during the 2018 outburst of the changing-look AGN (CL-AGN) NGC 1566. Numerical simulations indicate that the disk epicyclic oscillations responsible for high-frequency QPOs are damped by magnetohydrodynamic turbulence unless the accretion flow is misaligned and/or eccentric. In TDEs, the stellar debris stream is naturally misaligned with the SMBH spin, while NLS1s may host misaligned disks due to their youth. Motivated by the QPO candidate in NGC 1566, we propose that CL-AGN accretion is also misaligned -- potentially fueled by captured, free-falling broad-line region clouds. This model naturally explains why CL-AGN transition timescales are much shorter than the standard disk viscous timescale. This picture can be tested by searching for QPOs or quasi-periodic eruptions in other CL-AGN.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a QPO candidate in the CL-AGN NGC 1566 during its 2018 outburst, proposing misaligned accretion flows as a common motif among NLS1s, TDEs, and CL-AGNs fueled by captured broad-line region clouds, which shortens transition timescales and can be tested via further QPO observations.
AI.motivation: To understand the rarity of QPOs in AGNs and their association with specific systems like NLS1s and TDEs, identifying new QPO candidates in CL-AGNs to test accretion flow models involving misalignment or eccentricity.
AI.method: Analysis of X-ray timing data from NGC 1566's 2018 outburst to detect QPO signals, coupled with magnetohydrodynamic simulations showing QPO damping unless accretion flows are misaligned/eccentric.
AI.result: Discovery of a QPO candidate in NGC 1566, suggesting misaligned accretion in CL-AGNs consistent with NLS1s and TDEs, with accretion fueled by captured clouds.
AI.conclusion: CL-AGNs' rapid transitions are due to misaligned accretion from free-falling clouds, explaining shorter timescales than standard viscous disks. Future searches for QPOs in CL-AGNs can validate this model.

title: Parton distribution functions and theory parameters: an NNPDF perspective
comment: Submitted for publication by Elsevier Ltd. in a Physics Reports collection of papers on Electroweak Precision Physics, edited by F. Bedeschi, A. Kotwal M. Ramsey-Musolf, C. Vellidis, and D. Wackeroth
summary: Parton Distribution Functions (PDFs) are a key ingredient in theoretical predictions for Large Hadron Collider (LHC) observables and play a central role in the extraction of precision Standard Model (SM) and Beyond the SM (BSM) parameters from LHC data. Recent analyses demonstrate that the determination of fundamental SM parameters such as $α_s(m_Z)$, $m_W$, $m_t$, and $\sin^2θ_W$ is strongly influenced by the choice of input PDFs. In this contribution, we present the status and challenges of PDF determination from the NNPDF perspective, both in stand-alone fits and in joint extractions with (B)SM parameters. We place particular emphasis on results for $α_s(m_Z)$, $m_t$, and Wilson coefficients in the SM Effective Field Theory (SMEFT) framework.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses the importance of Parton Distribution Functions (PDFs) in determining Standard Model parameters (αs(mZ), mW, mt, and sin²θW) and Beyond the SM parameters at the LHC. It highlights the impact of PDF choice on precision measurements and presents the NNPDF approach for PDF determination, including joint fits with BSM parameters like SMEFT Wilson coefficients.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges in achieving precise theoretical predictions and accurate extractions of SM and BSM parameters from LHC data, which are significantly affected by variations in PDFs.
AI.method: The NNPDF methodology is used for PDF determination, including stand-alone fits and joint extractions with (BSM) parameters. The analysis focuses on results involving αs(mZ), mt, and Wilson coefficients in the SMEFT framework.
AI.result: Results demonstrate that PDF uncertainties significantly influence SM parameter determinations. The NNPDF approach provides a framework for robust PDF and parameter extractions, particularly in SMEFT analyses.
AI.conclusion: PDFs are critical for precise LHC physics. The NNPDF method offers a reliable tool for simultaneous determinations of PDFs and (BSM) parameters, essential for advancing LHC analyses in both SM and BSM contexts.

title: An investigation on the FWHM of absorption features of type Ia supernovae
comment: accepted for publication in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics
summary: We present an investigation of the full width at half maximum (FWHM, or γ) of absorption features of Type Ia supernova (SNe Ia). We found that, the average value of FWHM can be well predicted with the rest wavelength (λ). The velocity also plays an important role, as objects with a higher velocity tend to have a larger FWHM. Temperature may be the third factor, as we found that, at the same velocity (but different phases), a normal-velocity (NV) object tends to have a larger FWHM than high-velocity (HV) object. Also, 1991T/1999aa-like objects that are believed to have relatively high temperatures show the largest FWHMs if compared at the same velocity. Generally speaking, FWHM evolves very slowly with time and shows no correlation with Δm15, but 1991T/1999aa-like objects are characterized by relatively fast decreasing FWHM. On the other hand, we found that, objects with relatively small FWHMs shows a tighter correlation between absorption depth (A) and Δm15, possibly a sign of higher degree of homogeneity. We also found that A/γ of Si II λ5972 has a strong correlation with Δm15, and more importantly, a relatively slow time evolution, making it a useful luminosity estimator even in the absence of phase information.

AI.tldr: The study investigates the FWHM of SNe Ia absorption features, finding dependencies on wavelength, velocity, temperature, and object type, with implications for luminosity estimation.
AI.motivation: To understand the factors influencing FWHM in SNe Ia and identify potential luminosity indicators without phase information.
AI.method: Analyzing FWHM values in SNe Ia spectra, investigating correlations with velocity, temperature, object type (e.g., 1991T/1999aa-like), and phase. Examined relationships between FWHM and Δm15, as well as A/γ of Si II λ5972.
AI.result: FWHM correlates with wavelength, velocity, and temperature; 1991T/1999aa-like objects show higher FWHMs. A/γ of Si II λ5972 correlates strongly with Δm15 and evolves slowly, making it a viable luminosity estimator.
AI.conclusion: FWHM behavior provides insights into SN Ia physics and homogeneity. Si II λ5972's A/γ offers a phase-independent luminosity tool, enhancing cosmological applications.

title: A Model for Dark Moments of the W Boson
summary: Loops of portal matter (PM) fields carrying both dark and Standard Model (SM) quantum numbers can lead to the kinetic mixing (KM) of the SM photon and the analogous dark photon (DP) of a phenomenologically interesting magnitude. However, in specific frameworks, different loops of these same PM fields can also lead to other new types of interactions between some of the SM fields and the DP via the generation of `dark moment'-like couplings, even though the SM fields carry a zero dark charge at tree-level. In recent work, this possibility has been explored for the case when these SM fields are fermionic. In this paper, we extend this idea to the case of the SM $W$ boson employing a previously examined model wherein PM consists of a complex scalar triplet plus a complex singlet, both of which obtain vevs and thus also generate the DP mass. While this setup results in a somewhat stronger interaction between the DP and the $W$ than in the familiar KM setup, the rate for $W+$DP production at the LHC is shown to still be rather small and is very difficult to observe due to large SM backgrounds. The direct production of the scalar PM itself, however, is shown to lead to similar new physics signatures but with much larger rates and are considered here in their own right. The properties of such new PM states may already be constrained by LHC searches in both the $W^\pm+$MET and $W^+W^-+$MET channels.

AI.tldr: This paper explores the interaction between dark photons (DP) and SM W bosons through portal matter fields, showing that while direct DP-W coupling is stronger than kinetic mixing, production rates at the LHC are low. However, portal matter scalar production offers observable signatures in W±+MET and WW+MET channels.
AI.motivation: To extend previous studies of dark matter interactions with SM fermions to the case of the W boson, exploring new interaction types via 'dark moment' couplings despite zero dark charge at tree-level.
AI.method: Using a scalar triplet and singlet portal matter model that obtains vevs to generate DP mass. Calculated interaction strengths and LHC production rates for DP-W systems versus direct production of portal scalars.
AI.result: DP-W coupling strength exceeds kinetic mixing models, but production rates remain too low for observability due to SM backgrounds. Direct portal scalar production provides viable signatures with higher rates, constrained by existing LHC searches.
AI.conclusion: Direct production of portal scalars offers more promising observational avenues than DP-W pairs. Existing LHC data in MET channels may already constrain PM properties, guiding future dark sector searches.

title: A NICER View of PSR J0030+0451: Updated Constraints from Six Years of NICER Observations
comment: Submitted to ApJ; comments and feedback are welcome
summary: Pulse-profile modeling of rotation-powered millisecond pulsars targeted by NICER has enabled mass--radius constraints of several neutron star sources, with implications for the dense-matter equation of state. For the bright isolated pulsar PSR J0030+0451, the inferred mass--radius was previously found to depend strongly on the assumed hot spot model. These hot-spot models yielded different mass--radius constraints, with the statistically preferred model exhibiting some mild tension with results inferred for PSR J0437$-$4715, PSR~J0614$-$3329, and GW170817. We present an updated pulse-profile analysis of PSR J0030+0451 using new NICER observations obtained between 2017 July to 2023 January, increasing the number of X-ray counts by about 50% compared to previous analyses. We jointly analyze the NICER data with archival XMM-Newton observations to better constrain the source spectrum and background. The new analysis significantly reduces the discrepancy between the hot spot models. The inferred mass and radius are $M = 1.43^{+0.20}_{-0.17}\,M_\odot$ and $R_{\rm eq} = 12.68^{+1.31}_{-1.04}$ km (68% credible intervals), reducing the tension with the results from other sources. In addition, the inferred hot spot configurations suggest the presence of intra-spot temperature gradients.

AI.tldr: The study updates the analysis of PSR J0030+0451's pulse profiles using new NICER and archival XMM-Newton data, reducing discrepancies between hot spot models and yielding mass-radius constraints (M=1.43 solar masses, R=12.68 km) that align better with other neutron star observations, while suggesting intra-spot temperature variations.
AI.motivation: To refine mass-radius constraints for PSR J0030+0451 by addressing previous model-dependencies and tensions with other neutron stars, using enhanced data sets and joint spectral analysis.
AI.method: Combined analysis of extended NICER observations (2017-2023) and XMM-Newton archival data to model pulse profiles, improve spectral fitting, and reduce reliance on hot spot model assumptions.
AI.result: Reduced tension in mass-radius results compared to other neutron stars; obtained M=1.43+0.20-0.17 Msun and Requ=12.68+1.31-1.04 km. Detected evidence for intra-spot temperature gradients in the pulsar's magnetosphere.
AI.conclusion: Improved data and analysis methods enhance consistency across neutron star equation-of-state measurements, suggesting that intra-spot temperature variations may influence observations and require more sophisticated hot spot models.

title: Kaons ($K^\pm$) in hot and dense QCD
summary: We present a systematic QCD sum-rule analysis of the in-medium properties of the charged kaon doublet $K^{\pm}$ over the full $(T,ρ)$ plane relevant to current and forthcoming heavy-ion experiments. Working within the QCD sum-rule framework and incorporating temperature-and density-dependent quark, gluon, and mixed condensates, we derive Borel-transformed sum rules for the effective masses $m_{K^{\pm}}$, the pseudoscalar decay constants $f_{K^{\pm}}$, and the vector self-energy $Σ_{v}$ of both charged states simultaneously. Our vacuum results, $m_{K^{-}} = 494.6^{+4.9}_{-6.9}$~MeV and $f_{K^{-}} = 157.3^{+4.1}_{-2.9}$~MeV (with near-degenerate $K^{+}$ values), are in excellent agreement with Particle Data Group values at the sub-percent level. In the medium, $m_{K^{\pm}}$ decreases monotonically with increasing baryon density and temperature, signalling progressive partial restoration of chiral symmetry. A pronounced mass splitting $Δm = m_{K^{-}} - m_{K^{+}}$ develops in baryonic matter, driven by the opposite sign of the Weinberg--Tomozawa vector interaction for the two charge states; it reaches $|Δm| \sim 0.35$~GeV near $ρ\simeq 3.2\,ρ_{\rm sat}$ at $T = 0$ and is partially quenched by thermal fluctuations. A central outcome of this study is the extraction of the critical onset density $ρ_c$, defined as the threshold beyond which the in-medium modifications of $K^{-}$ properties signal the onset of the transition toward the chirally restored phase. We stress that $ρ_c(T)$ should not be interpreted as a precise determination of the QCD critical point-a task beyond the reach of any current effective framework-but rather as an indicator ....

AI.tldr: The paper systematically investigates the in-medium properties of charged kaons (K±) using QCD sum rules across the full (T,ρ) plane relevant to heavy-ion experiments.
AI.motivation: To understand the in-medium behavior of K± and probe chiral symmetry restoration, specifically identifying the critical onset density (ρ_c) indicating the transition to a chirally restored phase.
AI.method: QCD sum-rule framework incorporating temperature/density-dependent condensates. Simultaneous derivation of Borel-transformed sum rules for K± effective masses, decay constants, and vector self-energies.
AI.result: Vacuum values match PDG data within ~1%. In medium: masses decrease with T/ρ, significant mass splitting (Δm ~0.35 GeV at high density) from Weinberg-Tomozawa interaction differences. Critical onset density ρ_c identified as indicator (not precise QCD critical point).
AI.conclusion: The analysis provides a systematic framework to study in-medium K± properties, highlighting their role as sensitive probes of chiral symmetry restoration phases. Results stress the importance of ρ_c as an experimental indicator requiring further validation in heavy-ion collisions.

title: Magnetic Flux Tubes Illuminated by Pulsar Winds
comment: 11 pages, 7 figures. Accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: Observations of linear structure connecting pulsars to gamma-ray halos reveal injection of TeV electrons into the interstellar medium (ISM). In some cases, this could be attributed to nearly scattering-free electron transport along large-scale magnetic fields connected to pulsar winds with very slow diffusion across the field lines. In this work we model this process with a magnetic flux tube emerging from the pulsar and attached to the ISM magnetic field. We show that particles in this case have an anisotropic distribution of magnetic pitch angle, such that the overall emission is highly beamed. We apply this model to pulsar tails and filaments, including the extended X-ray and TeV emission associated with PSR J1740+1000 and the misaligned X-ray jet in the Guitar Nebula, to constrain their particle population and magnetic fields.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the transport of TeV electrons from pulsars into the interstellar medium via magnetic flux tubes, explaining observed linear structures and gamma-ray halos through anisotropic particle distributions and beamed emission. It applies the model to specific neutron star systems to constrain their particle populations and magnetic fields.
AI.motivation: To understand the mechanism behind the observed linear structures connecting pulsars to gamma-ray halos, particularly the transport of high-energy electrons with minimal scattering, and to explain the beamed emission observed in pulsar tails and filaments.
AI.method: The authors model electron transport using a magnetic flux tube emerging from pulsars and interacting with the interstellar magnetic field. They analyze the anisotropic magnetic pitch angle distribution of particles and apply the model to specific cases like PSR J1740+1000 and the Guitar Nebula.
AI.result: The model shows that particles exhibit an anisotropic distribution leading to highly beamed emission, which explains observed structures. Constraints on particle populations and magnetic field strengths are derived for studied systems.
AI.conclusion: The flux tube model successfully accounts for observed pulsar-associated emissions by explaining anisotropic transport and beaming, offering a framework to study particle dynamics and magnetic interactions in pulsar wind systems.

title: Pion and $ρ$ meson's unpolarized quark distribution functions from $q\bar{q}$ and all Fock-states within Dyson--Schwinger equations
summary: We compute the twist-2 unpolarized quark parton distribution functions (PDFs) of the pion and the $ρ$ meson within the Dyson-Schwinger equations (DSEs) framework using the rainbow-ladder (RL) truncation. A new DSE for the spin-1 hadron's quark-quark correlation matrix is derived, from which the PDFs can be extracted. For the $ρ$ meson, we obtain for the first time within RL-DSEs the unpolarized PDFs corresponding to different helicity states. A pronounced difference is observed between the $|Λ|=1$ and $Λ=0$ cases, where $Λ$ denotes the meson helicity, leading to a nonvanishing and numerically sizable tensor-polarized PDF $f_{1LL}(x)$. We further compare these results with those obtained under a leading Fock-state ($|q\bar{q}\rangle$) truncation and find substantial deviations. This comparison demonstrates that the present RL-DSEs framework incorporates higher Fock-state contributions associated with gluonic degrees of freedom, which has a significant impact on the quark distributions.

AI.tldr: The paper calculates twist-2 unpolarized quark parton distribution functions (PDFs) for the pion and ρ meson using the Dyson-Schwinger equations (DSEs) framework with rainbow-ladder truncation. It derives a new DSE for spin-1 hadrons' quark-quark correlation matrix, obtaining the first RL-DSE-based unpolarized PDFs for the ρ meson's helicity states. Significant differences between Λ=1 and Λ=0 helicity cases are found, including a sizable tensor-polarized PDF. Comparisons with leading Fock-state truncation show substantial deviations, highlighting the role of higher Fock-state contributions from gluons in quark distributions.
AI.motivation: To explore quark distributions in mesons like the pion and ρ meson, and understand the impact of different truncation schemes (rainbow-ladder vs. leading Fock-state) on PDFs, particularly regarding helicity states and gluonic contributions.
AI.method: Deriving a new DSE framework for spin-1 mesons' quark-quark correlation matrix, solving DSEs with rainbow-ladder truncation to compute PDFs. Calculations compare results under RL-DSE and leading Fock-state truncations to analyze effects of higher Fock-state contributions.
AI.result: First RL-DSE-based unpolarized PDFs for ρ meson's helicity states. Notable differences between Λ=1 and Λ=0 cases, including non-vanishing tensor-polarized PDF f_{1LL}(x). Major discrepancies observed between RL-DSE and leading Fock-state truncation results, indicating significant gluonic/Higer Fock-state effects.
AI.conclusion: Rainbow-ladder DSE framework inherently includes higher Fock-state gluonic contributions, which profoundly affect quark PDFs. This contrasts with leading Fock-state models, emphasizing the necessity of full framework calculations for accurate meson structure studies.

title: The hydrodynamics of stratified ultra-relativistic outflows and the origin of GRB X-ray plateaus
summary: The origin of the X-ray plateau phase observed in a large fraction of gamma-ray burst afterglows remains debated. We present a novel analytic framework for the hydrodynamics of ultra-relativistic, radially stratified outflows interacting with an external medium. By explicitly accounting for a continuous distribution of Lorentz factors within the ejecta, we derive analytic expressions describing the evolution of a long-lived, mildly relativistic reverse shock and determine its crossing time. Then, we compute the resulting synchrotron emission from both the forward and reverse shocks. The forward shock naturally produces a shallow, long-lasting X-ray decay consistent with the observed properties of X-ray plateaus (including the Dainotti relation). We further show that reproducing the observed plateau durations requires the stratified ejecta to extend to Lorentz factors of order $\gtrsim 100$, consistent with the ultra-relativistic outflow that powers the prompt $γ$-ray emission. The reverse shock generates a long-lived millimeter emission component that outshines the forward shock emission at these wavelengths. Both the plateau and reverse shock emission terminate smoothly once the slowest ejecta are processed, marking a transition to the standard Blandford-McKee self-similar evolution without requiring late-time energy injection or an additional emission component. Such stratified outflows are expected on physical grounds, as the ultra-relativistic ejecta responsible for the prompt $γ$-ray emission are unlikely to be launched with a single Lorentz factor. This model provides a unified picture in which the same outflow powers the prompt emission, the X-ray plateau, and the subsequent afterglow evolution.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an analytic framework explaining the X-ray plateau phase in gamma-ray burst afterglows through a stratified outflow model, linking it to the prompt gamma-ray emission and standard afterglow evolution without needing energy injection.
AI.motivation: To resolve debates about the origin of X-ray plateaus and provide a unified model connecting the prompt gamma-ray emission with afterglow phases using physically motivated outflow structures.
AI.method: Developed analytic equations for ultra-relativistic, radially stratified ejecta interacting with external medium, accounting for variable Lorentz factors, and calculating emissions from both forward and reverse shocks.
AI.result: The forward shock reproduces X-ray plateaus (matching the Dainotti relation), reverse shock causes millimeter emission, and plateau duration requires ejecta with Γ~100. The model transitions naturally to standard afterglow without extra components.
AI.conclusion: Stratified outflows unify prompt emission, X-ray plateau, and afterglow, eliminating the need for late energy injection and confirming theoretical expectations for ejecta composition.

title: Two-zero textures of the Majorana neutrino mass matrix from $\mathbb{Z}_3$ gauging of $\mathbb{Z}_N$ non-invertible symmetry
summary: Texture-zero ansatze offer an economical description of neutrino masses, with current data allowing only seven inequivalent two-zero Majorana textures in the charged-lepton mass basis. We investigate how such textures can arise from non-invertible symmetries realized through $\mathbb{Z}_3$ gauging of $\mathbb{Z}_N$. In contrast to $\mathbb{Z}_2$ gauging, which necessarily induces diagonal neutrino mass terms via the Weinberg operator, $\mathbb{Z}_3$ gauging admits complex representations and allows a richer class of neutrino mass textures. If the light neutrino mass is described by the Weinberg operator, we find that the textures $\mathbf{A}_{1,2}$, $\mathbf{B}_{3,4}$, and $\mathbf{C}$ can be realized from the $\mathbb{Z}_{3}$ gauging of $\mathbb{Z}_{13}$ symmetry, while all the seven phenomenologically viable two-zero textures can emerge from $\mathbb{Z}_{3}$ gauging of $\mathbb{Z}_{19}$ symmetry without requiring supersymmetry. When the neutrino mass is generated by the type-I seesaw mechanism, the structure of the non-invertible symmetry is more restrictive, yielding only texture $\mathbf{C}$ for $N\neq7$. These results demonstrate the strong predictive power of non-invertible symmetries for neutrino mass textures. Furthermore, the more general $\mathbb{Z}_{n}$ gauging of the $\mathbb{Z}_{N}$ symmetry with $n>3$ is analyzed, which results in novel fusion rules.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how non-invertible symmetries via $\mathbb{Z}_3$ gauging of $\mathbb{Z}_N$ can generate neutrino mass textures, achieving seven viable two-zero textures with $\mathbb{Z}_{19}$ and specific textures with $\mathbb{Z}_{13}$, while type-I seesaw restricts to texture C.
AI.motivation: To investigate the origin of neutrino mass textures using non-invertible symmetries, particularly comparing $\mathbb{Z}_3$ versus $\mathbb{Z}_2$ gauging and their ability to produce allowed patterns without supersymmetry.
AI.method: Analyzes $\mathbb{Z}_3$ gauging of $\mathbb{Z}_N$ symmetries to derive neutrino mass textures via Weinberg operator and type-I seesaw mechanisms, examining constraints imposed by symmetry structures.
AI.result: Shows $\mathbb{Z}_{13}$ generates textures A₁₂, B₃₄, C via Weinberg operator; $\mathbb{Z}_{19}$ produces all seven viable textures; type-I seesaw only allows texture C for N≠7. Fusion rules expand with $\mathbb{Z}_n$ (n>3) gauging.
AI.conclusion: Non-invertible symmetries provide powerful predictions for neutrino textures, with $\mathbb{Z}_3$ gauging enabling richer structures than $\mathbb{Z}_2$, and $\mathbb{Z}_{19}$ symmetry explaining all observed textures without SUSY.

title: Multi-messenger emission derived from relativistic magnetized jet dynamics using a multi-zone framework
summary: Relativistic jets from Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) are highly energetic and emit radiation across a wide range of frequencies. Despite several observational studies, their particle composition still remains a key open question. The detection of high-energy neutrinos from blazar sources such as TXS 0506+056 has highlighted the plausibility of hadronic/lepto-hadronic models for AGN jets. To understand the origin of high-energy neutrinos from such sources, it is imperative to capture the complex interplay between the jet dynamics, their composition, and the mechanism of particle acceleration and cooling in relativistic jets. In this pilot study, we have coupled a numerical multi-zone framework for lepto-hadronic modeling, with 3D relativistic magneto-hydrodynamic simulations of AGN jets, including external photon fields. Our framework provides synthetic multi-wavelength and neutrino flux by spatially sampling the simulated jet into multiple zones. We investigate the implications of such a framework in exploring the different intrinsic and extrinsic pathways for proton-enrichment in jets. Essentially, we find that for low proton-to-electron number density ratios, producing a substantial jet neutrino flux, requires the underlying proton energy distribution to have a relatively flat spectrum with a power-law index of $\simeq 2.0$. We further find that while intrinsic shocks triggered by kink-instabilities in the jet can accelerate electrons to high energies, they may not be sufficient to produce such flat particle energy distributions for the chosen set of parsec-scale jet parameters. Finally, to produce a significant jet neutrino emission, our simulations suggest the need to consider particle acceleration mechanisms through alternative pathways, either internal or external.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a coupled numerical framework combining multi-zone lepto-hadronic modeling with 3D relativistic MHD simulations of AGN jets to explore neutrino production. It finds that flat proton energy spectra (index ~2.0) are required for significant neutrino fluxes, and intrinsic shocks from kink instabilities may be insufficient, suggesting alternative acceleration mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Determine the particle composition and acceleration mechanisms in AGN jets responsible for high-energy neutrino emission, particularly in blazars like TXS 0506+056, by integrating jet dynamics and multi-wavelength observations.
AI.method: Coupling a multi-zone lepto-hadronic model with 3D relativistic MHD simulations of AGN jets, incorporating external photon fields. The jet is divided into zones for synthetic neutrino and multi-wavelength flux calculations, analyzing proton-enrichment pathways.
AI.result: A proton-to-electron ratio of ~2.0 is needed for substantial neutrino flux. Intrinsic shocks from kink instabilities alone may fail to achieve the required flat proton spectra at the chosen parsec-scale parameters, indicating a need for additional acceleration processes.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the necessity for alternative proton acceleration mechanisms beyond intrinsic shocks in AGN jets to explain observed neutrino emissions and emphasizes the framework's utility in testing different particle population scenarios.

title: Fermion Mass Hierarchy and a High Quality Axion From Gauged U(1) Flavor Symmetry
summary: We present a class of models based on a gauged $U(1)_F$ flavor symmetry that explains the hierarchical structure of fermion masses and mixings via the Froggatt-Nielsen (FN) mechanism, while also solving the strong CP problem by the Peccei-Quinn (PQ) mechanism. A global $U(1)_{\rm PQ}$ symmetry with a nonzero QCD anomaly emerges accidentally in this setup as a byproduct of the gauged $U(1)_F$ symmetry. The resulting axion is shown to be of high quality, with the axion potential safeguarded against quantum gravity corrections by the gauge symmetry. Three models, which are generalizations of the Dine-Fischler-Srednicki-Zhitnitsky (DFSZ) axion model, are presented realizing this idea. The right-handed neutrino mass scale is identified as the Froggatt-Nielsen scale in these models. We present explicit UV completions of the FN sectors of these models and show that they preserve the high quality of the axion. In these models, the axion acts as a flavon field, leading to testable predictions in flavor-changing decays of neutral mesons. The axion also serves as the dark matter of the universe with the right amount of relic abundance without causing cosmological domain wall problems. Baryon asymmetry of the universe is realized via leptogenesis which is calculable in these models and found to be of the right order of magnitude.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a class of models combining gauged U(1)_F symmetry with the Froggatt-Nielsen mechanism for fermion masses and the Peccei-Quinn mechanism to solve the strong CP problem. The models generate a high-quality axion as dark matter, avoid domain wall issues, and link axion physics to flavor phenomena via testable meson decay predictions. Leptogenesis explains the baryon asymmetry.
AI.motivation: To address two fundamental problems: the hierarchical fermion mass spectrum and the strong CP problem. Existing solutions (FN for masses, PQ for CP) are unified here, ensuring a consistent framework that also naturally produces a cosmologically viable axion dark matter candidate.
AI.method: Building three generalized DFSZ axion models with a gauged U(1)_F symmetry. This symmetry accidentally generates a global U(1)_PQ with QCD anomaly, creating an axion protected from quantum gravity effects. The FN mechanism explains fermion masses via a hierarchical scale linked to the right-handed neutrino mass. UV completions ensure axion quality remains intact.
AI.result: Successful unification of fermion mass generation and strong CP solution with axion dark matter. Predicted flavor-changing meson decays offer testability. Leptogenesis provides a consistent baryon asymmetry explanation. Avoids domain wall problems and maintains axion's high-quality potential against corrections.
AI.conclusion: The proposed models elegantly merge flavor physics and axion cosmology, providing a testable framework addressing multiple beyond-Standard-Model puzzles. The interplay between gauged symmetries and accidental global symmetries opens new avenues for particle physics and cosmology.

title: Weak emission line quasar SDSS J101353.45+492758.1 I. Continuum fitting
comment: 11 pages, 13 figures, 4 tables, submitted to Astronomy & Astrophysics
summary: We present a broadband study of the WLQ SDSS J101353.45+492758.1, which displays a nearly featureless UV-optical spectrum with only a weak Mg II line alongside an exceptionally low X-ray flux. We model its spectral energy distribution using the relativistic thin-disk model kerrbb with a power law, and the multicomponent AGN model relagn, a physically motivated extension of agnsed incorporating warm and hot Comptonizing regions. Our fits constrain the black hole mass, accretion rate, X-ray loudness, and coronal energetics. Both approaches yield consistent BH masses of M_{BH} \approx 2 \times 10^{9} M_\odot and an Eddington accretion rate of \dot m \approx 0.1. The relagn fit including a warm Comptonizing region provides a significantly improved representation of the UV-soft X-ray continuum. The warm corona, characterized by kTe \simeq 0.20 keV, Γ \simeq 3.8, and an optical depth τ \simeq 7.26, extends to \sim 34 R_g. The hot corona appears compact and energetically suppressed, leading to an intrinsically weak X ray output with log(L_{X}/L_{bol}) \simeq -4.29, among the lowest reported for WLQs. The α_{ox} \sim 2.06 indicates the source to be in high/soft AGN spectral state. The combination of a luminous, standard disk and extremely weak hot corona suggests that this quasar hosts a highly inefficient inner coronal region. This explains its X-ray faintness and extreme deficit of high-ionization emission lines. The source may represent an AGN analog in "ultrasoft" accretion state, or a system in which the ionizing continuum is suppressed by a compact or quenched corona. Our study suggests that the source is not accreting at high Eddington ratio, highlighting the physical diversity of WLQs, and supports the view that geometric and radiative effects jointly shape their extreme spectral properties.

AI.tldr: The study analyzes the WLQ SDSS J101353.45+492758.1, finding it hosts a highly inefficient inner corona leading to weak X-ray emission and reduced high-ionization lines. This quasar's properties suggest an 'ultrasoft' accretion state or suppressed ionizing continuum, highlighting diverse WLQ physics.
AI.motivation: To investigate the physical mechanisms behind the X-ray faintness and lack of emission lines in warm, luminous quasars (WLQs), specifically addressing why they exhibit low X-ray flux despite high accretion rates.
AI.method: Modeling the spectral energy distribution using relativistic thin-disk (kerrbb) and multi-component AGN models (relagn), incorporating warm and hot Comptonizing regions. Fitting to observational data across UV-optical-X-ray wavelengths.
AI.result: Both models yield a BH mass ~2e9 solar masses and low Eddington ratio 0.1. The warm corona parameters (kTe, Γ, τ) and its extent (~34 R_g) explain weak X-rays (Lx/Lbol ~ -4.29). The low α_ox (~2.06) indicates a soft AGN state. The hot corona is compact and less energetic.
AI.conclusion: WLQs like this exhibit inefficient inner coronal activity, explaining their spectral peculiarities. They may represent extreme accretion states or coronal quenching, challenging earlier high-Eddington accretion assumptions and emphasizing combined geometric/radiative effects in their spectra.

title: NNLOCAL: Fully Local Subtractions for Precision Predictions in Hadron Collisions
summary: This work extends the CoLoRFulNNLO subtraction method to address soft and collinear divergences in the computation of higher-order corrections for hadronic collisions. By utilizing universal local counterterms which can be integrated analytically over the unresolved phase space, we achieve numerically stable, fully-differential predictions. Our publicly available NNLOCAL code serves as a proof-of-concept implementation, validated by calculating the NNLO cross-section for Higgs boson production in gluon-gluon fusion with no light quarks.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the CoLoRFulNNLO subtraction method to handle soft and collinear divergences in hadronic collisions, providing numerically stable predictions via universal local counterterms. The NNLOCAL code calculates Higgs boson production at NNLO with gluon-gluon fusion.
AI.motivation: Address divergences in higher-order QCD corrections for precise hadronic collision predictions, ensuring numerical stability and computational efficiency.
AI.method: Adaptation of CoLoRFulNNLO with universal local counterterms, integrated analytically over unresolved phase space, implemented in NNLOCAL code. Tested on Higgs production in gluon-gluon fusion without light quarks.
AI.result: Successfully produced fully-differential NNLO cross-sections for Higgs production, demonstrating numerical stability and code validation.
AI.conclusion: The method provides a reliable framework for automated NNLO computations in hadronic collisions, validated by Higgs production results and public code availability.

title: Unfolding without Iterations, Adversaries, or Surrogates
summary: Correcting measurements for detector effects and constructing appropriate public data representations is a pressing problem in LHC physics. Current methods solve this inverse problem by relying on iterations, minimax optimization, or a surrogate forward mapping. We introduce Adversary-free Unfolding SanS Iteration or Emulation (AUSSIE), which dispenses with these mechanisms while remaining asymptotically correct. AUSSIE replaces the second OmniFold step with a new loss function that directly yields solutions with minimal dependence on the reference simulation. We showcase AUSSIE on various unfolding tasks, including full-phase-space jet substructure.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AUSSIE, a new method for correcting detector effects in LHC physics data without iterations or surrogates, achieving asymptotic correctness with a novel loss function that reduces dependence on reference simulations.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of current unfolding methods (like iterative processes and reliance on simulations) by developing a more efficient and less simulation-dependent approach.
AI.method: AUSSIE replaces the second step of OmniFold with a new loss function that directly minimizes dependence on reference simulations, avoiding iterations and surrogate models while ensuring asymptotic correctness.
AI.result: AUSSIE successfully demonstrates improved performance in unfolding tasks, particularly in complex scenarios like full-phase-space jet substructure analysis.
AI.conclusion: AUSSIE offers a simpler, more reliable solution for detector unfolding in LHC experiments, reducing reliance on simulations and computational resources compared to existing techniques.
